entries.name: The di Errante Tragedy
entries.summary: The catastrophic event approximately 1,100 years ago in which the di Errante family's destruction — rooted in jealousy, a supernatural bargain, and a stolen identity — transformed the family's vast estate into the cursed fog-valley of Vallombrosa.

details.label: What Is Known Publicly
details.content: The di Errante estates on the western coast became Vallombrosa — a fog-shrouded valley that has never cleared. The di Errante family was destroyed. Legends about the cause vary widely. No one publicly knows the specific truth.

details.label: The Brothers
details.content: Marcello di Errante was the elder brother — powerful, accomplished, genuinely beloved, with fey-touched nature and shadow manipulation abilities. Lorenzo was the younger brother, driven by jealousy and entitlement, unable to accept that Isabella chose his brother freely.

details.label: Isabella Fortuna
details.content: A woman of considerable beauty who possessed genuine prophetic abilities. She loved Marcello freely and completely. She never chose Lorenzo of her own will.

details.label: Lorenzo's Bargain
details.content: Seven years before the wedding, Lorenzo sought out the Keeper of Faces and negotiated a bargain: supernatural power to coerce Isabella's love in exchange for 'the face you show the world.' He believed the price was metaphorical. The actual price was his literal identity, name, and sense of self — collected on the anniversary, making him appear as Marcello and unable to speak his own name.

details.label: The Sphinx Consultation
details.content: Shortly after making his bargain, Lorenzo consulted Tartuzi the sphinx, who told him the exact collection date and provided the basic principles for binding an Archfey. This consultation, given freely according to sphinx nature, led directly to Tartuzi's own millennia of enslavement.

details.label: The Wedding Day
details.content: Isabella hired an adventuring party (The Emerald Tide party) to prevent the Keeper from collecting the debt. What she didn't know: Lorenzo had orchestrated their arrival using her own prophetic abilities against her. The party performed a binding ritual using a mirror, compass, and hourglass — trapping the Keeper at the moment of legitimate debt collection.

details.label: The Cosmic Theft
details.content: With the Keeper trapped and helpless, Lorenzo drained power directly from the imprisoned Archfey, transforming himself into something far more than human. This collapsed the magical coercion sustaining Isabella's false love. She confronted Lorenzo in horror. When she could no longer love him, he murdered her with his stolen power.

details.label: The Final Battle
details.content: Marcello attacked Lorenzo in grief and rage. The brothers fought an evenly matched battle that destroyed the ceremonial gardens. Lorenzo killed Marcello. Tartuzi appeared — pulled by the completed binding mechanism to become the eternal lock of the prison. The adventuring party was knocked unconscious, imprisoned in the estate dungeon, and offered poisoned wine. Their ultimate fate is unknown.

details.label: The Curse
details.content: The combination of the Keeper's imprisonment, the stolen power, and the murders transformed the entire di Errante estate into Vallombrosa — a cursed fog-valley from which people emerge as fog children with no memories, where time moves strangely, and where the imprisoned supernatural forces still exert influence on the surrounding area.

connections.relationship: Was imprisoned during this event; remains imprisoned in Vallombrosa today
Became the eternal lock of the Keeper's prison during this event; has been trapped for a thousand years
The arriving Marcello is either the original Marcello or Lorenzo wearing his stolen identity — it remains ambiguous

entries.dm_notes: Players begin knowing distorted legend versions of this event. Discovering the true history is a major campaign-long investigative arc. The adventuring party (Emerald Tide) may be connected to PC reincarnations. The di Errante bargain with the Covenant (fragmentary records exist) may be relevant to understanding the full scope of what Lorenzo negotiated.

entries.name: Vallombrosa
entries.summary: The fog-shrouded valley covering the old di Errante estate, created by the ancient tragedy and now the source of fog children, supernatural phenomena, and the imprisoned forces that increasingly affect all of Venturia.

details.label: What Venturia Knows
details.content: Vallombrosa has existed for over a thousand years as a fog-shrouded valley where people occasionally emerge with no memories. Legends about what it is vary. Most citizens grew up with it as part of their landscape — spooky and mysterious but just there. Nobody seriously believes any specific version of the legends as historical fact.

details.label: The Fog Line
details.content: The boundary between Venturia and Vallombrosa is maintained by the Fog Wardens. The fog line has measurably moved over the years — sometimes closer to the city, sometimes farther — by inches or feet. The movement appears to be accelerating.

details.label: Strange Phenomena Near the Boundary
details.content: Lights moving in the mist that couldn't be torches. Voices in unrecognized languages. Figures visible just inside the boundary, standing motionless before fading. Extended exposure produces sleep difficulties, persistent feelings of being watched, and dreams about places the dreamer has never been.

details.label: What Vallombrosa Actually Is
details.content: The di Errante family estate, transformed by the imprisonment of the Keeper of Faces and the theft of its power. The fog is a manifestation of the supernatural forces contained within. The fog children who emerge are souls cycling through the liminal space — some were people who wandered in and emerged unchanged while decades passed outside. Others may be born within Vallombrosa.

details.label: The Prison Weakening
details.content: The Keeper's prison is weakening. Evidence: the fog line's accelerating movement, Silas's appearance, the increasing rate of supernatural oath-consequences, and the phenomena Teodor Vast's ships are experiencing at sea. The weakening will reach crisis point when Marcello arrives.

details.label: The Three Fanes
details.content: Three supernatural anchors within Vallombrosa maintain the imprisonment mechanism. Destroying them would affect the prison — possibly freeing the Keeper, possibly releasing Tartuzi, possibly triggering catastrophe. Each fane destroyed has escalating consequences. The party will discover at least one during Act One.

details.label: Vallombrosa in Act Two
details.content: After Marcello's arrival (Phase Two threshold), Vallombrosa transforms from an external mystery into a living setting the party can explore. It has a population of fog children (and others), internal locations, and a geography shaped by the ancient di Errante estate. The Tragedy of Vallombrosa performed at the Amaranth Theater is a distorted artistic echo of the actual events.

connections.relationship: Guard the boundary between Venturia and the fog
Has been receiving fog children from Vallombrosa for over a thousand years
Imprisoned within Vallombrosa; its weakening imprisonment drives the campaign's supernatural crisis

entries.dm_notes: Players know Vallombrosa as a spooky local legend. The reveal that it is an actual supernatural prison containing a still-living Archfey and a sphinx-turned-prison-lock should be genuinely revelatory. Phase One keeps it mysterious. Phase Two opens it as explorable territory.

entries.name: Fog Children
entries.summary: People who appear from Vallombrosa's fog with no memories of their lives before. They have been appearing in Venturia for over a thousand years, since the ancient tragedy. Liminal House was founded specifically to care for them.

details.label: What Venturia Knows
details.content: Fog children appear periodically from Vallombrosa with no memories. They are cared for by Liminal House. Noname's father was one. Orabella is one. The phenomenon is long-established and accepted as part of Venturia's unusual nature.

details.label: True Nature
details.content: Fog children are souls cycling through Vallombrosa's liminal space — the supernatural in-between created by the Keeper's imprisonment. Some are people who wandered into the fog years or centuries ago and emerged unchanged while time passed outside. Others may genuinely be reincarnated souls, reborn through the fog's connection to the space between lives.

details.label: Institutional Knowledge Suppression
details.content: Both Liminal House and Viro's Respite know more about fog children than they disclose to residents. When fog children ask about their experiences — particularly recurring first-person dreams of other lives — they are shut down. This suppression is deliberate and has been maintained across generations of institutional leadership.

details.label: Reincarnation Possibility
details.content: Orabella's recurring first-person dreams of being other people, and Valentro's muscle memory of sword fighting, may both indicate reincarnation — past lives surfacing through the fog's liminal properties. If some PCs are reincarnations of the original Emerald Tide adventuring party, this should emerge through play naturally rather than as a predetermined revelation.

connections.relationship: The institution founded to care for fog children
A fog child experiencing recurring first-person dreams of other lives
Noname's father was a fog child; Noname herself was raised at Liminal House after his death

entries.dm_notes: The reincarnation mechanic is a long-game revelation. Handle carefully — it should create meaningful character moments and moral complexity, not feel like a plot twist imposed on players. Strange dreams, recognizing places they've never been, knowledge they shouldn't have — these should emerge naturally through play.

entries.name: The Autumn Masquerade
entries.summary: Venturia's most important annual cultural event, centered on the Amaranth Theater's staging of 'The Tragedy of Vallombrosa.' A city-wide masquerade tradition that carries genuine supernatural weight during the week before it occurs.

details.label: Public Significance
details.content: The most anticipated cultural event on the island. The Amaranth Theater stages a different director's interpretation of the Vallombrosa legend each year. The city debates productions for months. Everyone wears masks. The Masquers' Sodality is deeply involved in organizing the traditions.

details.label: Car's Trauma
details.content: Car witnessed a friend making a secret deal with an unknown third party before the Masquerade. The next night, she found her friend's body dead at the event. Her parents claim it was 'betrayal' but won't explain further.

details.label: Ferrier Prohibition
details.content: The Ferriers' Confraternity refuses to ferry passengers after midnight during the week before the Masquerade. Those who navigate the canals alone during those nights report becoming hopelessly lost as waterways seem to shift and fold until dawn.

details.label: Supernatural Significance
details.content: The Autumn Masquerade appears to coincide with a supernatural cycle connected to Vallombrosa's prison mechanics. The week before the Masquerade, the canals become genuinely dangerous. The theater itself feels 'more alive' during staging of the Tragedy. These are likely connected to the Keeper's domain (masks, identity) and the annual cycle of the prison's weakening or strengthening.

details.label: Silas's Plans
details.content: Silas has some agenda that climaxes at or around the Autumn Masquerade. What he is building toward and why the Masquerade is significant to his plans is an unresolved campaign question.

connections.relationship: Site of her friend's death; ongoing trauma and investigative thread
The Tragedy of Vallombrosa is staged here annually during the Masquerade
Silas has plans that climax at or around the Masquerade

entries.dm_notes: The Masquerade is a strong Act One climax target — natural deadline for Silas's plans and a spectacular setting for major revelations. The canal behaviors during Masquerade week suggest it's a thin-boundary period when the supernatural is closer to the surface. Orabella may have been at the Masquerade when Car's friend died — explore this connection.

entries.name: The History of Eldoria
aliases: The Age Before the Great Reunion
entries.summary: The broader world history of Eldoria: a civilization sealed from the wider world for nearly five thousand years by the consequences of leaders who invented fake gods to reduce divine influence, and the Great Reunion that ended that isolation a generation ago.

details.label: The Sealing
details.content: Approximately 5,000 years ago, the rulers of Eldoria invented nine fake gods (the Church of the Nine) to reduce real divine influence over mortal affairs. Millions directing belief toward nonexistent gods weakened the true deities, who withdrew. The seas around Eldoria became impassable. Eldoria was sealed from the wider world of Eldyrin.

details.label: The Church of the Nine
details.content: For nearly five millennia, the fabricated religion was the dominant cultural and political force. The true gods were reduced to rumor. The Church did genuine good — hospitals, law, libraries — built on fraudulent theological foundations. The true deities worked quietly through the gaps, channeling power to those who believed themselves to worship false gods.

details.label: The Last Wrongs
details.content: An adventuring party called the Last Wrongs stood against Vecna's plot to bring a demon lord back to the material plane. Heroes from Faerûn (the wider world) crossed into the sealed continents and destroyed Vecna. In the aftermath, the Church of the Nine attempted to consolidate power, but the Last Wrongs and their allies exposed the Church's fundamental fraud.

details.label: The Great Reunion
details.content: When the Church's power collapsed, the true gods returned. The seas cleared. Ships from Faerûn and other regions sailed in. Eldoria discovered it had been isolated from a vastly larger world. The Great Reunion is a decade-long process of first contact, cultural shock, and theological reorientation that ended the sealed age.

details.label: Relevance to Venturia
details.content: The campaign is set in the post-Reunion era. The island of Seravalle and Venturia exist in this changed world where true deities have returned and the wider world is newly accessible. Garl Glittergold's direct interaction with Roxy reflects the restored divine presence. The Keeper of Faces is a pre-Reunion supernatural force whose imprisonment predates the world's current configuration.

entries.dm_notes: The world history provides context for why divine patrons (Garl, the Keeper, Tartuzi's sphinx nature) function as they do. The restoration of true divine power is recent — the world is still adapting. The Keeper's imprisonment predates the Church, predates the Reunion, and predates everything the current civilization takes for granted.

entries.name: The Bound
entries.summary: Individuals in Venturia who have been supernaturally compelled — controlled without their consent. The Bound are appearing with increasing frequency, causing deaths, and driving the Council's current crisis response that has produced only gridlock.

details.label: What Is Known
details.content: Bound individuals appear throughout Venturia, compelled to act against their will. One Bound individual caused six deaths in a single incident. They are not criminals — they are victims. But they are also walking weapons regardless of intent.

details.label: Council Gridlock
details.content: The Bound Response Debate is a prime example of council dysfunction: Harbor-Lords want detention and examination. Guild-Masters want mandatory paid leave and care. Temple Authority wants sanctuary. Watch-Captain wants permanent detention. Nothing passes. The Watch detains them under vagrancy laws, causing protests. The problem escalates.

details.label: True Source
details.content: The Bound are created through supernatural compulsion connected to Silas and/or the weakening prison mechanics. Silas has the ability to create Bound individuals. The prison weakening may also cause spontaneous Bound phenomena without Silas's direct involvement.

details.label: Covenant's Perspective
details.content: The Covenant of Silver Scales is particularly disturbed by The Bound — supernatural compulsion represents the ultimate violation of free agreement, the antithesis of everything they believe about contract and consent.

connections.relationship: Silas creates Bound individuals; this is connected to his memory manipulation abilities

entries.dm_notes: The Bound are both a symptom and a threat — they represent the prison's deterioration and Silas's activities simultaneously. The council gridlock around them is realistic and frustrating, providing space for the party to act outside official channels. The Covenant's concern about free agreement vs. compulsion is a philosophical thread worth developing.

entries.name: The Prison's Collapse — Three Drains
entries.summary: The Keeper's prison is failing from three simultaneous degradation curves that have finally reached their intersection point after a thousand years. The collapse is not one catastrophe but three compounding failures.

details.label: Drain One — The Original Wound
details.content: Lorenzo didn't drain the Keeper like emptying a cup — he excised something from the Keeper's fundamental nature. The Keeper has been imprisoned with a wound that cannot close, because the stolen power is outside his reach and sustaining someone else. Under ordinary circumstances, an Archfey recovers from power loss by drawing on their domain. But the domain itself is failing.

details.label: Drain Two — Domain Collapse
details.content: Archfey are sustained by being what they are, which requires their domain to be alive and functioning. The Keeper needs a court where identity is celebrated and performed. The Pale Carnival fractured the moment he was imprisoned, and without his governing intelligence, it degraded. As it degraded, his ability to draw sustenance from it degraded with it. He has been in slow starvation for a thousand years, trying to recover from Lorenzo's wound using a domain that is itself dying.

details.label: Drain Three — The Fanes
details.content: The fanes are not just storage for stolen power — they are a continuous mechanism, routing residual power from the Keeper to stabilize Marcello's stolen abilities on an ongoing basis. Lorenzo built a pipeline. Stolen Archfey power is fundamentally unstable in a mortal vessel; without constant feed from its source, it degrades. Each fane governs a different aspect of that stabilization. So every year for a thousand years, the fanes have drawn a slow continuous trickle from an already-wounded prisoner who cannot replenish what he loses.

details.label: Why Now Specifically
details.content: Three separate degradation curves have finally reached their intersection point. The Keeper's original reserves are genuinely exhausted. The Pale Carnival has degraded past the point of providing meaningful sustenance. The fanes themselves are accumulating structural wear after a thousand years. And Marcello, upon returning to Vallombrosa to investigate his weakening power, instinctively began spending more time at the binding site where his abilities are strongest — increasing the draw on the Keeper, accelerating the very deterioration he came to investigate. He has been making it worse since he arrived.

details.label: Destroying a Fane — The Double Edge
details.content: Destroying a fane weakens Marcello but also accelerates the Keeper's deterioration — you're removing a regulated channel, causing remaining draws to become more erratic and more damaging. The party cannot simply destroy all the fanes and expect the Keeper to recover. The regulated pipeline, even as it slowly drains him, is also preventing the remaining draws from being catastrophically destabilizing. This is a genuine moral and tactical dilemma.

connections.relationship: The prisoner whose prison is collapsing under these three simultaneous drains
His ongoing presence at Vallombrosa accelerates the Keeper's deterioration; destroying fanes weakens him but destabilizes the prison
The continuous mechanism routing stolen power from the Keeper to stabilize Marcello's abilities
Its degradation is Drain Two — the Keeper cannot draw sustenance from a fractured domain

entries.dm_notes: The three-drain framing is important for communicating urgency without making the collapse feel arbitrary. Each drain has a visible cause players can trace. The fane double-edge is the central tactical tension of Phase Two: doing the obvious thing (destroy the fanes) has a non-obvious cost that players should discover through investigation, not be told upfront.

entries.name: The Fanes
entries.summary: Physical mechanisms built by Lorenzo di Errante to stabilize stolen Archfey power in a mortal vessel. Not passive storage — an active continuous pipeline drawing residual power from the Keeper to prevent Marcello's stolen abilities from degrading. Each governs a different aspect of that stabilization.

details.label: What They Are
details.content: Stolen Archfey power is fundamentally unstable in a mortal vessel. Without constant feed from its source, it degrades. Lorenzo recognized this and built the fanes as a stabilizing mechanism — a continuous pipeline routing residual power from the imprisoned Keeper to Marcello, preventing the stolen power from collapsing under its own instability.

details.label: What They Do to the Keeper
details.content: Every year for a thousand years, the fanes have drawn a slow continuous trickle from an already-wounded prisoner who cannot replenish what he loses. This is Drain Three of the prison's collapse. The fanes are not a minor factor — they are a slow-acting poison that has compounded across a millennium.

details.label: Destroying a Fane
details.content: Destroying a fane weakens Marcello (that aspect of stolen power becomes unstable) but also accelerates the Keeper's deterioration. You remove a regulated channel, causing remaining draws to become more erratic and more damaging. The regulated pipeline, even as it slowly drains the Keeper, is also preventing the remaining draws from being catastrophically destabilizing. This is a genuine tactical dilemma, not a clear-cut win.

details.label: Each Fane Governs a Different Aspect
details.content: Lorenzo divided the stabilization function across multiple fanes, each governing a different aspect of the stolen power. The specifics of what each fane governs should be developed — ideally tied to the Keeper's domain (masks, identity, reflection, glamour). Destroying each one degrades a specific facet of Marcello's power.

connections.relationship: The fanes stabilize his stolen Archfey power; destroying them weakens him but at a cost
The fanes drain him continuously; destroying them removes regulated channels and may worsen his deterioration
The fanes are Drain Three of the three-drain collapse mechanism

entries.dm_notes: The fane locations and what each one governs need to be designed. Tying each fane to one of the Keeper's domain aspects (masks, identity, reflection, glamour) would make destroying them feel thematically meaningful. As each fane is destroyed, Marcello loses a specific power type, which should be perceptible to players who know what the Keeper's domain covers.

entries.name: TL and Silas — The Prison's Inner History
entries.summary: The history of what happened inside the Keeper's prison over the thousand years of its existence — TL's transformation, Silas's true nature, their relationship, Silas's exploitation of TL, and the murder that enabled Silas's escape.

details.label: Timeline
details.content: The binding happened approximately 1,000 years ago, but TL and Silas's story begins much earlier. Both were already inside the Reverie Solenne when the binding happened. Fey courts don't experience time like the material plane — TL and Silas may have been imprisoned for 2,000–4,000 years in material plane time. Neither aged. Neither had a biological clock. Time, for them, was an incomprehensible span of existence without the texture mortal life gives it.

details.label: What TL Became
details.content: TL entered as a mortal scholar who had been transformed by a sphinx warlock pact. Millennia of fey exposure inside the Reverie Solenne completed that transformation. By the time the binding happened, TL wasn't mortal at all — something that had once been mortal, still carrying mortal memories and emotional patterns, but whose actual nature was closer to a minor fey intelligence sustained by a sphinx's power. He remembered being human the way a very old person remembers childhood: vividly, emotionally, but from an enormous distance.

details.label: What Silas Was
details.content: Silas was a lower deity or powerful divine trickster who had lost a cosmic contest with the Keeper long before Lorenzo was born. The Keeper imprisoned him, stripped him of most of his divine portfolio, and branded him as property. His true form inside the prison was something grand and terrible — a presence that made the prison's walls resonate uncomfortably. He performed the mild, meek old man because the prison suppressed his true nature and the disguise was useful.

details.label: Why Tartuzi Couldn't Warn TL
details.content: Tartuzi, as the lock, could perceive everything within the prison — including Silas's true nature. She knew exactly what Silas was and couldn't warn TL without alerting Silas that she was watching. She communicated with TL across the lock-threshold, sharing knowledge of the prison's structure, but the one thing she couldn't safely communicate was the danger in the room with him.

details.label: The Exploitation
details.content: Silas recognized that TL had accumulated genuine understanding of how the prison mechanism worked through his years of communication with Tartuzi. This knowledge was TL's death sentence. Silas spent decades cultivating TL's trust — the patience of a being who had already waited thousands of years and could afford to wait decades more. He learned what he needed to know about the mechanism. Then he acted.

details.label: The Murder and Escape
details.content: When Silas had learned enough, he murdered TL and used his death as a distraction or door to facilitate his own escape. The mechanism of the escape is deliberately left open. Tartuzi perceived it in real time, unable to intervene, unable to warn — only able to witness. Her inability to act despite perceiving everything is one of the cruelest aspects of her position.

details.label: What Remains
details.content: Tartuzi is now the lock of a prison she chose to enter for someone who is no longer in it, holding a prisoner she has no particular desire to protect (the Keeper), unable to either escape or die peacefully. The prisoner she wanted to protect was murdered by the prisoner she was watching. This is the central wound of her character.

connections.relationship: The scholar whose knowledge Silas exploited and whose murder enabled the escape
The escaped prisoner whose divine nature, patient deception, and murder of TL are the core of this history
The lock who chose to enter the prison for TL, communicated with him across the threshold, and witnessed his murder unable to act
His prison is the setting for this entire history; his original imprisonment of Silas created the situation

entries.dm_notes: The mechanism of Silas's escape — what exactly he did with TL's death — is left open for DM development. Options: TL's death created a momentary fluctuation in the lock-consciousness that Silas used as a door; TL's residual sphinx-power discharged at death and Silas had prepared to ride that discharge out; the murder itself was a ritual Silas had been slowly building the components for across the decades. Any of these can be true.
